title: Comparison of travel behaviour of two individuals with different tracking methods

Comparison of travel behaviour of two individuals with different tracking methods

Supervisors:

Imprint
Recommended Citation:
Amelia HeidChristopher Elsener (June 21, 2026). Comparison of travel behaviour of two individuals with different tracking methods. Zurich University of Applied Sciences, Departement Life Sciences and Facility Management, Institute of Natural Resource Sciences.
Keywords: movement analysis, google timeline, GPS,

title: 1  Abstract
text:
1  Abstract



This study compares the mobility behavior of two individuals tracked over few weeks using two different methods: Google Timeline and a GPS logger app. Using spatio-temporal trajectory analysis in R, movement parameters were calculated, including travel distance, speed, time away from home, commuting time to ZHAW, and road type usage via spatial overlay with OpenStreetMap data. Results show similar average speeds and commute times for both individuals, though one (Amelia) shows higher mean distances and time away from home, driven by outliers, while the other (Christopher) travels more consistently and primarily by train. A key finding concerns data quality: GPS logger data offered higher spatial precision but required substantially more cleaning effort due to app crashes and noise, whereas Google Timeline data was easier to process but suffered from low temporal resolution, leading to inaccurate road type classification.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2  Introduction



This project investigates the movement behavior of two individuals. A tracking of their movement was conducted using different tracking methods, one using GPS-based app (Christopher) and one using Google Timeline [1] (Amelia). Descriptive spatio-temporal trajectory analysis are applied to compare the mobility behavior. The analysis focuses on movement trajectories as sequences of timestamped geographic coordinates and further spatial analysis will include distance calculations, home-based radius analysis, and spatial overlay with OpenStreetMap [2] road network data to conclude frequently used road types. The interpretation of the results are supported by the participants’ own knowledge of their daily movements.
The following research questions get analysed:

Which road types are most frequently used based on the recorded trajectories?
How far is the average or maximal movement radius from their home when they travel?
How far do they travel on average and at what speed?
How long are the individuals out of their home on average?
How long is the commuting time from home to ZHAW?

As there are differences in data structure, sampling intervals, GPS accuracy and preprocessing effort in both datasets, an additional objective is to evaluate how different tracking methods influence the quality and reliability of trajectories, movement analysis and processability.



References


[1] “Google Maps - Google Timeline.” Accessed: Jun. 17, 2026. [Online]. Available: https://www.google.com/maps/timeline


[2] “OpenStreetMap,” OpenStreetMap. Accessed: Jun. 17, 2026. [Online]. Available: https://www.openstreetmap.org/

title: 3  Material and Methods
text:
3.1 Data
The following methods and data were used in this project. AI tools were used to support text refinement and to assist with more advanced coding tasks.
This project uses two primary mobility data sources, tracked from two individuals: Google Timeline data and GPS Logger data. Google Timeline provides sparse but semantically enriched location records, including latitude/longitude coordinates, timestamps, and partially pre-classified activity labels (e.g., walking, driving, cycling). However, its temporal resolution is low and irregular, which limits fine-grained movement analysis. The Coordinates are not as precise as GPS data and can deviate several meters from the known location. Over a tracking period of 17 days (08.04.2026 to 24.04.2026), a total of 5’420 points were recorded, of which 658 points were attributable to movement-related activity.
In contrast, GPS Logger data offers high-frequency, continuous tracking at fixed time intervals, containing raw coordinates and timestamps. This allows for detailed trajectory reconstruction but introduces noise, tracking holes (crashing app) and requires preprocessing.

title: 3  Material and Methods
text:
3.2 Used R packages
The project is implemented in R [1], [2] using a combination of data wrangling, time-series handling, spatial analysis, and cartographic visualization packages. The tidyverse and lubridate packages get used for cleaning, restructuring, filtering, summarising the movement data and management of time data. Spatial processing is mainly carried out with the sf package. OpenStreetMap road network data is accessed using osmdata and linked to the mobility trajectories through nearest-feature matching. For visualisation, ggplot2 gets used for statistical plots and the package tmap and leaflet will be used to display the movement data on maps. Other packages used for processing or plotting include; jsonlite, dplyr, purrr, stringr, osmextract, data.table.

title: 3  Material and Methods
text:
3.3 Pre processing

3.3.1 google timeline data

Importing Google Timeline JSON data, extracting coordinates, timestamps and convert them, as well as delete errors.
Calculating distances and time differences between two consecutive points. From these, the moving window is computed as the mean of both neighboring points.
Detecting stationary and moving phases using distance-based thresholds (< 100 m) and segment trajectories.
Estimating relevant locations such as home and ZHAW with most frequent coordinates in certain time frames.



3.3.2 GPS data:

title: 3  Material and Methods
text:
3.4 Parameter calculation

Preparing the use of OpenStreetMap and its categories which includes loading the Swiss road/rail network lines, filtering to relevant categories, grouping these categories and match the points to the nearest feature.
Calculating movement parameters:
Simple calculations for speed, time out of home, travel distances and travel time to ZHAW
Road type as time share per road type group
Visualising trajectories and movement patterns using maps and plots.

title: 3  Material and Methods
text:
3.5 Validation
The validation of the different calculated parameters and the road type matching with OpenStreetMap is based on methods described by [3]. The used methods include visualization and event validity. As the tracking data was collected by the authors, they do have knowledge of the correct events and can assign errors and estimate miscalculated values.

title: 4  Results
text:
4.1 Amelia’s movement
After moderate pre processing effort, the google timeline data over more than two weeks looks like in Figure 4.1. Blue are the movement points and red are the static points. As some of the trajectories have little data points, the travelled distances and speed are likely to be underestimated.
The other parameters are calculated once per day and once an average over the whole tracking period. In Figure 4.2 it is shown, that the parameters vary widely at different days.

title: 4  Results
text:
4.3 Comparison
When comparing the movement behavior of Amelia and Christopher, several similarities emerge. Both travel at a similar average speed, suggesting comparable ratios of train/vehicle use versus walking. Commuting times to ZHAW are also similar on average. Amelia travels slightly more on average, both in total distance and time spent outside the home, though this is strongly influenced by a few outliers that raise the mean. Regarding maximum travel radius, Christopher appears to travel the same distance more consistently, as his mean value lies at the top of the boxplot’s interquartile range.



Comparison of movement parameters of Amelia and Christopher.


By examining the road types used, the likely mode of travel can be estimated. “Rail” corresponds to train travel, “main road” typically indicates bus or passenger vehicle use (and in some cases cycling), while “local road” may correspond to walking, cycling, or driving. Comparing the time shares spent on different road types reveals clear differences between the two. Christopher appears to travel primarily by train, whereas Amelia spends more time on roads in general (note that some points may be misclassified).



Share of time on the road types of Amelia and Christopher.

title: 5  Discussion
text:
5.1 Google Timeline data
In the google timeline data, the accuracy is stated to be up to several hundred meters. Therefore the matching with roadtypes is inaccurate. An example of road type mismatch is the commuting from home to ZHAW and back in Figure 5.1. From Rapperswil train station to Wädenswil train station, only the train was used. But in the road type matching, several points are not matched as ‘Train’ and sometimes even far away from the railroads.
Another attempt to evaluate the accuracy of the road type matching is to have a look at the from google stated activities at these points. As they include more than the coordinates of the points (acceleration etc.) in their analysis, it is estimated to be more accurate. The percentages of different road types within the different google activities is shown in Figure 5.2.
At a first glance, some of the road types seem to be matched very wrong, but some of them can somehow be explained. The 26% of walking on the rail might be the changing of trains at train stations, the tram line is inside of the main road and therefore 68% matched with it. Nevertheless, a lot of the other percentages (e.g. cycling on rail or train on local road) cannot be explained other than being errors.

title: 5  Discussion
text:
Figure 5.1: Example of mismatched road types on the way from home to ZHAW (only train from Rapperswil to Wädenswil).










Figure 5.2: Percentage of different road types within the google matched activity.

title: 5  Discussion
text:
5.2 Processing efforts and accuracies
Cleaning and filtering the GPS data proved considerably more time-consuming than processing the Google Timeline data. Within buildings and tunnels, GPS measurements frequently deviated substantially from the actual position, and repeated app crashes introduced multiple gaps in the dataset. Furthermore, the high sampling frequency of the GPS data resulted in an extremely large number of recorded points, further increasing processing effort. In contrast, speed and travel distance calculations based on the Google Timeline data were less accurate, as the sampling interval ranged up to several minutes. Additionally, the positional accuracy of the Google Timeline data was generally lower than that of the GPS data.
